text: Exercise-induced dehydration increases rating of perceived exertion by 0.21 points on the Borg scale for each 1% increase in body mass loss.
specific_value: 0.21 RPE points per 1% body mass loss

text: The effect of exercise-induced dehydration on perceived exertion is unlikely to be practically meaningful until body mass loss reaches at least 3%.

Within 0.5-3% body mass loss from dehydration, the maximum difference in RPE compared to euhydration is 0.81 points on the Borg scale, which is below the 1-point threshold for practical meaningfulness.
Humidity, ambient temperature, and aerobic fitness level do not alter the relationship between exercise-induced dehydration and perceived exertion.

Mild to moderate dehydration (up to ~3% body mass loss) during endurance exercise does not meaningfully increase how hard the effort feels, suggesting athletes need not aggressively prevent all fluid loss during training.
